content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
( 916) 322-5660 • Fax ( 916) 322-0886 
Chris Becnel, JD CPA 
Finance Director 
City of Clearlake 
14050 Olympic Drive 
Clearlake, California 95422 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-097 
Dear Mr. Becnel: 
May 17, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Clearlake City Councilmember 
Joyce Overton regarding the conflict ofinterest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 
and Section 1090. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and Section 1090, and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions that could apply 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1099 (related to public officers simultaneously 
holding two public offices that are incompatible). 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Lake County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 (c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c)(S).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Is Councilmember Overton' s position as Executive Director of the Highlands Senior 
Services Center (HSSC) a conflict, under Section 1090 or otherwise, with her position as a 
Councilmember of the City of Clearlake? 
2. Can any conflict be resolved by the Councilmember's abstention from any decisions 
regarding the HSSC Organization or must she recuse herself from any decision regarding the 
Community Center Building? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 2 
3. If the Staff of the HSSC takes over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee, will that cause a conflict of interest for the Councilmember? 
4. If the answer to 3 above is yes, then if the Councilmember is excluded from any part of 
that leasing management process will that eliminate the conflict? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Merely holding a private sector position is not in itself a conflict of interest. However, 
Councilmember Overton will have a conflict of interest under both Section 1090 and the Act in 
decisions regarding the contract with HSSC, as discussed below. 
2. Both the conflict under the Act and the conflict under Section I 090 require the 
Councilmember to abstain from any decisions regarding the HSSC contract. 
3. If the Councilmember does not participate in the agreements, the City may still enter into 
an agreement with HSSC to have them take over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee. However, the Councilmember must abstain from the decisions. 
FACTS 
Councilmember Joyce Overton is a Councilmember in and for the City of Clearlake and has 
been a Councilmember since December 2004. You stated in a communication dated May 12, 2016 
that the Councilmember has authorized you to request this advice. 
Clearlake is a general law City in the State of California fonned in 1980.Clearlake owns a 
building (Community building) constructed in or about 2003 and HSSC, a nonprofit, 50l(c)(3), 
rents a portion of the building during the hours of 7am -Spm. The building has always been leased 
to the HSSC. The current lease provides for rental to HSSC and allows for the City to rent the 
building during specified hours, evenings and weekends. 
The tenn of the current lease started on May 12, 2011 and is a IO-year lease ending in 2021. 
As a provision of that lease, the City is allowed to rent out the Community Center, or portions of it, 
for events by other organizations including the City. The City through its staff currently arranges 
and retains the rental proceeds from all leases of the building. 
The current rental rate for HSSC is $1,735.57. The rate is adjusted each year starting July I, 
2012 based upon the March to·March U.S. "All Urban Cities Consumer Price Index." The previous 
adjustments were overlooked for years 2012, 2013 and 2014. 2015 did not have an adjustment. The 
rate has now been adjusted to what it should be. The prior unpaid adjustments totaling $2,503.93 
were waived by the city. 
During the negotiation of the latest lease, Ms. Overton was a Councilmember for Clearlake. 
Overton was not an officer, director, employee, or volunteer for the senior center at the time the 
lease was negotiated. In approximately July of 2015 Joyce Overton became the compensated 


File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 3 
Executive Director of the HSSC while retaining her post as a councilmember for the City of 
Clearlake. The HSSC is governed by a volunteer Board of Directors. The Directors are charged 
with employing the Executive Director and evaluating her performance based on the overall success 
of the organization. 
Any rentals that the City arranges are currently made through the City by City Staff. During 
that process it is not unusual for Ms. Overton, or a HSSC staff member, to show the building to 
potential renters. This process is considered cumbersome and a proposal has been made to allow the 
HSSC staff to show the property, arrange the paperwork, collect the rents, and remit proceeds to the 
City. The HSSC would receive a fee for the work they would now be performing on the City's 
behalf. 
The current rental rates charged to the public for use of the Community Center were 
modified last year and in most cases were reduced so as to make the Community Center more 
affordable for the public. Ms. Overton did not participate in the discussion or the vote to modify the 
rates. 
ANALYSIS 
Co11jlicts of lllterest under the Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. The Act's conflict of interest provisions only apply to conflic;ts of 
interest based on the following: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, oŋ holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d)) 
• Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $460 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103( e ). ) 


File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 4 
• In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or 
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
You stated that Councilmember Overton is employed by HSSC, a 501(c)(3) public benefit 
corporation. While a nonprofit is not a "business ßntity" for purposes of Section 87103(a) and (d) 
(see Section 82005)1 it will still be considered a source of income to the Cot!ncilmember because 
she receives $500 or more annually from HSSC. (Section 87103(c).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality: Having concluded that HSSC is a potentially disqualifying 
source of income to the Councilmember, the next step in the conflict of interest analysis is to 
determine if the specific governmental decisions will have a foreseeable and material financial 
effect on the source of income. 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is 
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is 
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Similarly, Regulation 18702.3 provides in a pertinent part: 
"(a) Income from the Sale of Goods and Services: For income received by 
the official or his or her spouse for goods and services provided in the ordinary 
course of business, including a salary, the financial effect is material if: 
"( 1) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or 
is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding ... " 
Under your facts, HSSC will be "explicitly involved" in decisions related to the lease and 
performance of the lease. Under such circumstances, the effect is presumed to be foreseeable and 
material and the Councilmember will have a conflict of interest under the Act. 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of 
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the 
discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details 
of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave 
the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 
18707.) 
Sectio11 I 090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 


File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 5 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all ·parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649 .) Moreover, the prohibition is not necessarily cured by abstention. As a city councilmember, 
participation in a contract or contract modification is presumed under Section 1090, regardless of 
whether the officer actually participates in the decisions. If a councilmember is financially 
interested in the contract, and no exception applies, Section 1090 prohibits the contract from being 
made by the agency at all. 
1. Is Councilmember Overton subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. Councilmember Overton is an officer and an employee of a 
local government agency and is subject to Section 1090. (See e.g., Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633.) 
2. Does the decision involve a contracl? 
To detennine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
(1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351.) A decision to amend a contract constitutes 
involvement in the making of a contract under section 1090. ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191.) Even if a separate agreement between the City and HSSC was reached 
to have HSSC essentially be the property manager for a fee, this would involve the making of a new 
contract subject to Section I 090. Oral contracts are also subject to Section 1090.2 
3. Is the official mal-.ing or participating in making a contract? 
Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the 
making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974} 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, for purposes 
of Section 1090, participating in making a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and 
specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae 
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) You 
have specifically asked whether the Councilmember may participate in the new proposed agreement 
with HSSC so we proceed to the next step. 
2 For example, an offer of employment with a public agency by the appointing official and the acceptance of 
such employment by the appointee constitutes the making of a contract within the meaning of Section 1090 even though 
there is no written contract. (36 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 121, 122 (1960).) 


4. Does the official have a financial interest in the contract? 
File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 6 
Under Section l 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest" (People v. Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 333), and officials are deemed to 
have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) 
Although Section 1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as 
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of 
pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, 38 Cal.3d at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga 
(1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Teny v. Bender (1956) 1 43 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People 
v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
In addition, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the term 
"financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deyslzer (1934) 2 Cal.2d 
141, 146 ["(h)owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the 
forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) Further, 
"the certainty of financial gain is not necessary to create a conflict of interest . . .  (t)he government's 
right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the 
officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass 
(2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
Here, the Councilmember is a compensated Executive Director of HSSC. Generally, the 
executive director is granted authority by the board of the organization to run the organization 
consistent with the best interests of the organization and is accountable to the board of directors. 
The agreement would increase HSSC's revenue which would benefit the councilmember who is 
evaluated on the overall performance of the board of the company. This role as compensated 
Executive Director of HSSC constitutes a financial interest that could prevent the Council member 
from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of the City. 
5. Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
There are several statutory exceptions to the prohibition in Section 1090. The remote 
interests in Section 1091 apply only to the members of a body or board, and do not apply to 
employees. Generally, if a remote interest exception applies, the officer shall not be deemed to be 
interested in a contract entered into by a body or board of which the officer is a member within the 
meaning of this article if the officer has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of that 
interest is disclosed to the body or board of which the officer is a member and noted in its official 
records, and thereafter the body or board authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith 
by a vote of its membership sufficient for the purpose without counting the vote or votes of the 
officer or member with the remote interest. (Section 1091(a).) 
Section 1091 (b) provides: 
"As used in this article, 'remote interest' means any of the following: 


File No. A-16-097 
Page No. 7 
"(I )  That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from 
taxation pursuant to Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. 
Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 50l(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 
U.S.C. Sec. 50l(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation, except as provided in 
paragraph (8) of subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5." 
Thus, based on this exception, the Councilmember can abstain and the City may enter into 
the agreement without her participation. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
sistant General Counsel, 
Legal Division

regarding the HSSC Organization or must she recuse herself from any decision regarding the 
Community Center Building? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in3. If the Staff of the HSSC takes over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee, will that cause a conflict of interest for the Councilmember? 
4. If the answer to 3 above is yes, then if the Councilmember is excluded from any part of 
that leasing management process will that eliminate the conflict?

2. Both the conflict under the Act and the conflict under Section I 090 require the 
Councilmember to abstain from any decisions regarding the HSSC contract. 
3. If the Councilmember does not participate in the agreements, the City may still enter into 
an agreement with HSSC to have them take over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee. However, the Councilmember must abstain from the decisions.

Executive Director of HSSC constitutes a financial interest that could prevent the Council member 
from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of the City. 
5. Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
There are several statutory exceptions to the prohibition in Section 1090. The remote 
interests in Section 1091 apply only to the members of a body or board, and do not apply to 
employees. Generally, if a remote interest exception applies, the officer shall not be deemed to be 
interested in a contract entered into by a body or board of which the officer is a member within the 
meaning of this article if the officer has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of that 
interest is disclosed to the body or board of which the officer is a member and noted in its official 
records, and thereafter the body or board authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith 
by a vote of its membership sufficient for the purpose without counting the vote or votes of the 
officer or member with the remote interest. (Section 1091(a).) 
Section 1091 (b) provides: 
"As used in this article, 'remote interest' means any of the following:  
"(I )  That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from 
taxation pursuant to Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. 
Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 50l(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 
U.S.C. Sec. 50l(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation, except as provided in 
paragraph (8) of subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5." 
Thus, based on this exception, the Councilmember can abstain and the City may enter into 
the agreement without her participation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is Councilmember Overton' s position as Executive Director of the Highlands Senior 
Services Center (HSSC) a conflict, under Section 1090 or otherwise, with her position as a 
Councilmember of the City of Clearlake? 
2. Can any conflict be resolved by the Councilmember's abstention from any decisions 
regarding the HSSC Organization or must she recuse herself from any decision regarding the 
Community Center Building? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in3. If the Staff of the HSSC takes over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee, will that cause a conflict of interest for the Councilmember? 
4. If the answer to 3 above is yes, then if the Councilmember is excluded from any part of 
that leasing management process will that eliminate the conflict?

CONCLUSION: 1. Merely holding a private sector position is not in itself a conflict of interest. However, 
Councilmember Overton will have a conflict of interest under both Section 1090 and the Act in 
decisions regarding the contract with HSSC, as discussed below. 
2. Both the conflict under the Act and the conflict under Section I 090 require the 
Councilmember to abstain from any decisions regarding the HSSC contract. 
3. If the Councilmember does not participate in the agreements, the City may still enter into 
an agreement with HSSC to have them take over the responsibility for renting of the facility to 
outsiders from the City for a fee. However, the Councilmember must abstain from the decisions.
